data.title: Intel: Buy Into The Pullback
data.description:
Introduction

Intel Corporation (INTC) has been one of the most polarizing companies available on the market as I’ve rarely seen a firm benefiting from such tailwinds in such a short time, as highlighted by the Nvidia (NVDA) investment. With the company releasing its latest earnings, I want to see if it is still worthy of a Buy rating, as it has appreciated nearly 42% since I last reviewed it in October.

Current Dynamics

There is a lot to decipher from the company’s recent earnings, and I’ll start with the basics. Intel posted a Q4 non-GAAP of 15 cents, a beat of $0.07 based on revenues of $13.7B, a beat of $310MM. Now you might be wondering why the company is dropping like a stone pre-opening, and that might have been the result of its lackluster forecast and manufacturing problems.

The Q1 projections came well below what Wall Street was estimating, as apparently “Chipzilla” is still struggling with supply chain bottlenecks and a shifting market landscape. Meaning that the production issues have continued to hamper the comeback attempt by Intel, which has heavily disappointed investors who were anticipating a boost from new products. Right now, it is suffering from low yields, which made it harder to fill orders, which is disappointing considering that INTC has spent years trying to restore its technological edge and recover from the loss of its dominant market share. The available supply will hit its lowest point in Q1, pointing to a difficult period ahead, especially with the shortage of memory chips.

Diving into the segments, Data Center & AI revenue increased by 9% Y/Y to $4.7B, which is the bright spot as demand for server CPUs alongside AI accelerators has surged, but Intel mentioned that it couldn’t keep up with the volume, which is both a huge tailwind as well as a disappointment in terms of manufacturing. Client Computing saw revenue decrease to $8.2B as the hype around AI PCs was not enough to offset slower sales, though Intel remains optimistic about the rollout of its new Core Ultra Series 3 under Panther Lake architecture. And finally, Intel Foundry, which has been the core of the pivot for the company, is highly important for the current pivot. Revenue was up 4% Y/Y to $4.5B as it is underway on the 18A process now in high-volume manufacturing in Arizona and Oregon.

Nvidia and Intel also closed their transaction, adding a $5B liquidity boost, helping Intel to fund its ambitious factory expansion. CEO Lip-Bu Tan also emphasized that Intel is weeks/months ahead of TSMC (TSM) in the high-volume production of 2nm-equivalent chips (18A), which is the key for the company to regain market leadership.

Overall, despite the barrage of negatives, I still believe that this was a good quarter for Intel, as it proved in a way that it can still manufacture world-class chips and secured a massive partnership with Nvidia. They have officially exited the emergency phase and entered the growth phase of their plans. They simply don’t have enough factory capacity to turn that into profit yet. Plus, the problem is that based on current financials, Intel has a much larger room to fall if it continues to disappoint shareholders.

Valuation

Due to the current turnaround, general metrics like P/E won’t tell us the whole story about Intel, as analysts are currently pricing the stock on the potential 2027-2028 recovery. By the time the 18A factors are fully ramped, earnings are expected to jump; on a 2028 forward basis, Intel is trading at a fairly reasonable 23x earnings, though it requires investors to wait two years for the payoff. There is a case that Intel’s business should be valued as two different businesses, Foundry and Products. On a bull case, the Foundry could be valued as a national champion akin to TSMC, with my estimate of the foundry assets being worth north of $150B. But right now, Intel is a story stock as the current valuation is not supported by cash flows but by all the deals and its prime position in chip manufacturing.

Looking ahead, I’m particularly enthusiastic about the fact that the company is now technically ahead of TSMC in high-volume production of 2nm-equivalent chips. Right now, Panther Lake is using backside power delivery, which is making it much more efficient than current laptop chips. Clearwater Forest, which is the server equivalent of the 18A node, aimed at taking back the data center market share that AMD’s EPYC chips have been chipping away at. Intel is also betting that geopolitical tensions will force American companies to shift production to U.S. soil. Intel is currently courting mega clients for its 14A node, with key decisions supposed to come in H2 2026, giving us a lot of clarity about the trajectory ahead. Lip-Bu Tan also set the bold target of 45% market share in the AI PC segment, with more than 200 AI-enabled laptop designs launching during the year.

Intel currently has a margin problem, that’s for sure. Current gross margin is standing at 37.9% following the recent quarter, which is fairly low for a leading-edge semiconductor company. That’s mainly due to the company paying double as they are spending billions to build new factories while still paying competitors to build some of the company’s current chips, such as Lunar Lake, until they are ready to manufacture everything themselves. Foundry is currently a massive money pit as it reports an operating loss of around $2.5B per quarter, and that’s why Lip-Bu Tan implemented a welcome fiscal discipline regime, with divestments and OpEx reduction keeping the company from bleeding out during the 18A ramp-up. The company is still targeting 60% gross margins and 40% operating margins by 2030. But to get to all of that, Intel needs to bag a mega-whale foundry customer and hit its manufacturing yields target.

Risks

The biggest risk would come from execution as the company has proven that yes, it can design 1.8nm chips, but can it mass-produce them profitably? The yields are in line with internal targets, but they are vastly below the industry standard needed for high profitability. If the yields do not improve rapidly, the company will continue to lose money on every wafer it produces. Any further delay for the 18A ramp and the upcoming 14A node would cause a massive exodus of investors who have priced in a flawless execution.

There is also the current cash burn problem, as the company is roughly spending $16-$18B a year on massive factories in Ohio and Germany, for example. The company’s FCF has been deeply negative for several quarters. And though it has stabilized its balance sheet, there is now very little room for error.

Conclusion

I still believe that Intel is worth investing in, considering that the company has done what many thought was impossible by catching up to TSMC. They are still lagging behind profitability and production-wise, but that is why the company has received so much interest in it, it’s a long-term play that requires patient investors and in the end pays off. I keep my buy rating on Intel.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: December Readers ID'd 9 Ideal 'Safer' Dividends In 40 Dogs
data.description:
Reader Selections

Since May, 2017, any dividend-paying stock mentioned in a message, e-mail or comment to the author has been fair game for a REader FAvorite listing in this series of articles. Thus, It is possible that only ROgues and discontinued, or dreadful, doubtful, dividend issues may appear.

Most valuable for the writer, however, are reader comments that catch errors in calculations or changes in direction, and figures to communicate. Examples include the reader who missed my "safer" dividend follow-up articles because they contain dividend payout ratios.

However the main subject of these articles are your stock ticker comments whether they be about favorites or rogues. Be sure to mention a ticker when you comment

The “safer” lists are now available on the regularly published side of Seeking Alpha but that has added to confusion, since my IDEAL stocks come in two flavors: (1) not so safe and (2) safe, which is preferable since the dividends are paid from cash flow, not borrowed money.

In June a reader asked if Qualified dividend stocks could be flagged. YCharts is taking that request up with their product development team. YCharts, says: “At this time we do not have a filter or screener criterion to identify stocks that explicitly pay "qualified dividends" per IRS standards. However, I am happy to pass along your feedback to our team if this is something that can be added on. I can't guarantee any changes, but they use feedback like that to help determine what enhancements should be added in future updates.”

My ten-year collection of Dogs of The Week portfolios are reserved for members of my Seeking Alpha Dividend Dogcatcher investing group subscribers and a celebratory anniversary book is forthcoming.

Still, every month readers have grumbled that they can’t find my ten Dog of the week portfolios in my Dividend Dogcatcher service on the SA Marketplace (now called Investing Groups) site.

Summary and reference guides to 11 portfolios:

Here is an updated SA listing of all my indexes to portfolio postings in my Dogcatcher Investing Group, by date:

In Investing Groups site:

October 3, 2016 for I

October 13, 2018 for II

October 7, 2018 for III

September 3, 2018 for IV (Ivy)

September 8, 2019 for V (Volio)

September 14, 2020 for VI (Vista)

October 10, 2021 for VII (Viital)

October 8, 2022 for VIII (Viking)

October 7, 2023 for IX (Ignition)

October 11, 2024 for X (Attention)

October 6, 2025 for XI (Elephant)

Incidentally, My Ignition (DOTW IX) portfolio launched October 6, 2023, had all its selections in place by October 4, 2024 and posted its last return on October 3, 2025.

The Attention Portfolio (DOTW X) was filled October 10 2025 and will be fully funded by October 9, 2026.

The Elephant Folio (DOTW XI) initiated October 6. 2025 will be filled with 52 member stocks in October 9, 2026, and be fully funded by October 8, 2027.

Stay tuned for the first newly formatted Toy Dog Portfolio of tiny but mighty low priced dividend dogs

Last winter a reader asked what the XN rating in the Market Cap column of my Yield and Target Price charts means. Here is my summary of those codes:

Dogcatcher Market Cap Codes

L= $10B+

M= $2B-$10B

S= $200M-$2B

XN=<$200M

Your comments count and encourage better writing by the dogcatcher.

An observation from April 2023:

“Mr. Arnold, I enjoy reading your many articles and get some great ideas from them. However I find the number of charts are a bit of overkill, many duplicating the info. I'm sure you have your set format and reasons why, but I'd be happy with only two charts; your sort by target gains and your sort by div yield. It would make it a much easier read. Thank You —pmbrandt 15Apr2023”

I’d gladly just submit those two charts and ditch all the verbiage. However, fifteen years ago when I started submitting articles, the SA editorial team said they needed text to go with the graphics and topsy was born.

Still, there are frequent complaints of too many words for little information. In May somebody thought I was paid by the word. No such luck

In May. 2025 a reader, named Move on, commented on my Bloomberg Focus List article, “Death by data…just get to the point already…

My reply: @Move on Good point! Here's a hint: Forget the text. Read the charts and graphs. Those are the dots to connect

In January 2025 I got in trouble with readers when I neglected to define an acronym used to describe the Ben Graham Value collection. Unintroduced, I dropped a GASV acronym on them which stood for “Graham All-Star Value” stocks, dogs, or pack.

No doubt, less is more! I’ll be alert for methods to make my Dividend Kings, Dow Dogs, Aristocrats, Fortune 500 Industry Leaders, MoPays, and all my other articles, less obfuscating.

In February, 2022 one reader suggested an option strategy for monthly-paying dividend stocks:

"You should identify where options are available on the Monthly dividend stocks. What I do is find mo-pay stocks with options, I buy and write covered calls about 6 - 12 months out. I look for a scenario where I collect the dividend and get my stock bought back at a much lower price than I pay, but pocket a premium that makes up the loss. This gives me a dividend boost, since my cost is lower. It's like a guaranteed CD with little risk."

[See a summary of ten fair-priced December ReFa/Ro Dogs (below actionable conclusion #21 mid-article) and sixteen “safer” choices in the Afterword, at the bottom of this article.]

More than one writer has decried my favoritism for low-priced stocks. They especially dislike my "ideal" stocks whose dividend returns from a $1k investment equal or exceed share price.

A prime example is Sirius XM Holdings Inc (SIRI); the satellite radio and pandora music catalog owner, spun off from Liberty media (and now listed as one of the S&P500).

A reverse-split re-priced SIRI in October 2024. Recently (1/22/26) priced at $20.66, SIRI still easily passes my test (of dividends from $1k invested exceeding share price) with a forward dividend yield of 5.26%! A little under $1k investment now buys 48.4 shares. and they'll throw the owner a return (from that $1k invested) over 2.3 times the share price. SIRI, however, is in the doghouse this month because of negative return for the year of -2.92%.

Assuming all things remain equal, SIRI dividends alone will pay back their purchase price in about 20 years (and that assumes the satellite radio and subscription music services can survive that long)!

In December 2023, a reader suggested my share price equal or less than the income from a $1k investment was too low. I agree with him that opportunities are lost and that many strong stocks exist at higher prices. For example, even the dividend from $10k invested in NVIDIA Corp (NVDA) could not buy one share of that stock. Plainly nobody buys NVDA for the dividend, just growth. (On 1/9/26 $1.00 of NVDA dividend was priced at $4,587.16)

However, I was reminded of a comment in May, 2024 that some readers take “dividends from one $1K invested” to mean the annual return from just one share. To clarify: if the single share price of a stock is $20 and the annual yield is 3.00%, the dividend paid from $1.000.00 invested is $30.00 or 1.5 times the single share price.

February 17, 2025 reader wdjaxOn responded to my suggestion that besides waiting for the market prices to drop or higher dividends to be declared for stocks, that stock splits could also make dividends from $1k invested exceed the single share price of the stock.

Stay tuned. I now include stock splits in my suggestions to those who determine the price of dividends.

Here is a useful reader report from CEFreeman.com as of May, 2025:

“Interesting! Not only have I discovered I am an accidental Income Investor, but evidently I am also a Dog Catcher. If I am understanding this correctly. I don't have $1000 to invest each time, but I strive to buy enough of something so the dividends always buy 1 share or more. Usually, I try to buy 100 shares. Sure, the positions I buy are very low priced, but I must be doing something right. I am growing my income solidly, penny by penny! Dog Catching Income Investor. I like it.”

The point of this ReFa/Ro article is: “Be More Dog!” Stay tuned, Toy dog stock breeds may be coming for you

Actionable Conclusions (1-10): Brokers Expect 25.77% To 52.74% Net Gains From Top-Ten ReFa/Ro Dogs By December, 2026.

Five of ten top ReFa/Ro Dogs (tinted gray in the chart below) were among the top ten price gainers for the coming year based on analyst 1-year target prices. So, this December, 2025 yield-based forecast for ReFa/Ro dogs, as graded by Wall St. Wizard estimates, was 50% accurate.

Estimated dividend-returns from $1000 invested in the ten highest-yielding stocks and their aggregate one-year analyst median target prices, as reported by YCharts, created the 2025-26 data points for the projections below. (Note: one-year target-prices estimated by lone analysts were not applied.) Ten probable profit-generating trades projected to December, 2026 were:

Franklin BSP Realty Trust (FBRT) was projected to net $527.41, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 5 analysts, less broker fees. A Beta number was not available for FBRT.

FinVolution Group (FINV) was projected to net $509.81, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 8 analysts, less broker fees.The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 67% under the market as a whole.

Alliance Resource Partners (ARLP) was projected to net $362.23 based on the median of target prices estimated by 3 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 68% less than the market as a whole.

Seven Hills Realty Trust (SEVN) was projected to net $342.94, based on annual dividends, the median of target price estimates from 3 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 49% under the market as a whole.

Westlake Chemical Partners (WLKP) was projected to net $303.46, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 41% less than the market as a whole.

Energy Transfer (ET) was projected to net $300.09, based on dividends, plus median target price estimates from 20 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 34% less than the market as a whole.

AT&T (T) was projected to net $293.11, based on the median of target price estimates from 25 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 62% less than the market as a whole.

Eagle Point Credit Co Inc (ECC) was projected to net $282.68 based on dividends, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 53% less than the market as a whole.

Verizon Communications (VZ) was projected to net $276.56, based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 24 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 66% under the market as a whole.

TotalEnergies SE (TTE) was projected to net $257.70 based on dividends, plus the median of target price estimates from 2 analysts, less broker fees. The Beta number showed this estimate subject to risk/volatility 81% less than the market as a whole.

The average net gain in dividend and price was estimated at 34.55% on $10k invested as $1k in each of these top ten ReFa/Ro stocks. This gain estimate was subject to average risk/volatility 62% less than the market as a whole.

The Dividend Dogs Rule

Stocks earned the "dog" moniker by exhibiting three traits: (1) paying reliable, repeating dividends, (2) their prices fell to where (3) yield (dividend/price) grew higher than their peers. Thus, the highest yielding stocks in any collection became known as "dogs." More precisely, these are, in fact, best called, "underdogs".

The December, 2025 ReFa/Ro By Total Returns

The December, 2025 ReFa/Ro By Analyst Targets

The November, 2025 ReFa/Ro By Yield

December ReFa/Ro Top 10 Stocks By Yield Ranged 9.04% To 29.27% Per YCharts

Top ten ReFa/Ro Dogs occupied three of eleven Morningstar sectors plus ETFs.

Two ETF’s placed first and third: EaglePoint Credit Co [1] and NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF (QQQI) [3]

Five financial representatives placed second, and fourth, through seventh: Orchid Island Capital (ORC) [2], Franklin BSP Realty Trust [4], Seven hills Realty [5], AGNC Investment Corp (AGNC) [6], Ellington Financial (EFC) [7].

Two energy members placed eighth, and ninth, TORM Plc (TRMD) [8], and Alliance Resource Partners (ARLP) 9]. Finally, one materials member took tenth, Westlake Chemical[10].

Dividend Vs. Price Results

The top pie chart above shows the relative strengths of the top ten December ReFa/Ro dogs by dividend and price as of market close 1/16/202

For December nine top-ten ReFa/Ro dogs claimed ideal status because their share prices are lower than the projected annual dividends from $1k invested in them.

A dividend dogcatcher priority is to select stocks whose dividends from $1K invested are greater than their single share prices. As mentioned above, that condition was reached by nine of the top ten.

40 ReFa/Ro Costs per Dividend Dollar as of 1/16/26

The lower chart reveals 30 more ReFa/Ro Dividend dogs subjected to the same test: divide the price of each stock by the annual dividend. Thus is calculated the cost per dividend dollar for each stock.

The least expensive dog of the December ReFa/Ro was Eagle Point Credit at $3.42 per dividend dollar and the most expensive was Finward Bancorp (FNWD) at $74.81.

Actionable Conclusion (21): Thirteen of Forty ReFa/Ro Showed Negative Free Cash Flow Margins

A negative gap between dividend yield and free cash flow yield defines a cash-poor stock. That is, their dividend payout exceeds their cash on hand to pay dividends.

The Thirteen cash poor are: Eagle Point Credit; Orchid Island; NEOA Nasdaq-100; Franklin BSP Realty, Seven Hills Realty Trust, AGNC Investment Corp, Ellington Financial, Enterprise Products Partners, United Parcel Service, Kinder Morgan, Duke Energy, The Southern Co, American Electric Power.

Remember this dogcatcher yield-based stock-picking strategy is contrarian. That means rooting for (buying) the underdog is productive when you don't already own those stocks. If you do hold those stocks, then you must look for opportune pull-backs in price to add to your position to best improve your dividend yield. Plenty of pull-back opportunities appear to be ahead. Dread the Fed, executive over-reach, tariffs, uncertainty, wars, confusion, and intimidation, too.

Actionable Conclusions: (22-31) Broker 1-Yr. Targets Showed 19.57% To 46.60% Top Ten ReFa/Ro Upsides To December 2026; (32). Four -0.57% to -11.99% Downsiders Trailed

To quantify top-dog rankings, analyst median price-target estimates provided a "market-sentiment" gauge of upside-potential. Added to the simple high-yield "dog" metrics, analyst median-price target-estimates provided another tool to dig-out bargains.

Analysts Forecast A 26.69% Advantage For 5 Highest-Yield, Lowest-Priced of 10 December ReFa/Ro Dogs As of 12/12/25 Data

Ten-top ReFa/Ro dogs were culled by yield for this monthly-update. Yield (dividend / price) results as verified by YCharts did the ranking.

As noted-above, top-ten ReFa/Ro dogs selected 1/16/26 by yield, represented three of eleven sectors (plus ETFs).

Actionable Conclusions: Analysts Expected 5 Lowest-Priced of Ten Highest-Yield 24.14% Vs. (35) 23.28% Net Gains by All Ten, Come December, 2026

$5000 invested as $1k in each of the five lowest-priced stocks in the top ten ReFa/Ro Dividend kennel by-yield were predicted by analyst 1-year targets to deliver 3.71% more gain than from $5,000 invested in all-ten. The fourth lowest-priced top ten yielding stock, Franklin BSP Realty Trust, showed top analyst-estimated gains of 57.74%.

The five lowest-priced ReFa/Ro top-yield dogs for December were: Eagle point Credit; Orchid Island; Seven Hills Realty, Franklin BSP Realty and AGNC Investment Corp with prices ranging from $5.74 to $11.93.

Five higher-priced ReFa/Ro top-yield dogs as of December were: Ellington Financial; Westlake Chemical; TORM Plc; Alliance Resource Partners; NEOS Nasdaq-100 High Income ETF with prices ranging from $14.01 to $54.36.

The distinction between five low-priced dividend dogs and the general field of ten reflected Michael B. O'Higgins' "basic method" for beating the Dow. The scale of projected gains based on analyst targets added a unique element of "market sentiment" gauging upside potential. It provided a here-and-now equivalent of waiting a year to find out what might happen in the market.

Caution is advised, however, since analysts are historically only 15% to 85% accurate on the direction of change and just 0% to 15% accurate on the degree of change.

As the Holiday Seasonal euphoria in the markets dies and after Santa failed to rally, the Wall Street moguls slack work, the feckless Fed-fueled slide and dithering presidential pronouncements gain momentum into 2026 with the exception of very few hot meme stocks, look for most of the ReFa/Ro Dogs to become Fair-priced… slowly, but surely, or suddenly, with an assist from trade war clouds, or a pandemic of one sort or another.

Afterword

Buy Any of 9 IDEAL from 16‘Safer' December ReFa/Ro Dogs

Of the 40 ReFa/Ro, 16 were tagged "safer" for dividends as they showed positive one-year returns and free-cash-flow-yields greater-than dividend-yields.

These Nine ”Ideal” candidates from the “safer” group showed dividend yields from $1,000 invested exceeding their single share prices: TORM Plc; Western Midstream Partners (WES); MoDiv Industrial (MDV); Slate Grocery REIT (SRRTF); Verizon; Altria Group (MO); Pfizer (PFE); AT&T (T); Tsakos Energy Navigation (TEN)

Contributors:

Here is the full pack of 40 November ReFa/Ro

(Listed alphabetically by ticker symbol, the pack includes the nicknames of recommending readers.)

Stay tuned for future Dividend Dogcatcher Re/Fa/Ro Updates, out here, in public, away from the pay-walled Seeking Alpha Investing Group site (formerly known as the Marketplace).

The net gain estimates mentioned above did not factor-in any foreign or domestic tax problems resulting from distributions. Consult your tax advisor regarding the source and consequences of "dividends" from any investment.

Stocks listed above were suggested only as possible starting points for your "Safer" ReFa/Ro dog dividend stock research process. These were not recommendations.

Disclaimer: This article is for informational and educational purposes only and should not be construed to constitute investment advice. Nothing contained herein shall constitute a solicitation, recommendation or endorsement to buy or sell any security. Prices and returns on equities in this article except as noted are listed without consideration of fees, commissions, taxes, penalties, or interest payable due to purchasing, holding, or selling same.

Graphs and charts were compiled by Rydlun & Co., LLC from data derived from Indexarb; YCharts; finance.yahoo.com; analyst mean target price by YCharts. Open source dog art from dividenddogcatcher.com.

data.title: Tempus AI: Staying Bullish On Leading Healthcare AI Play
data.description:
Tempus AI, Inc. (TEM) has been one of the more resilient AI companies across the market. After a strong stock performance in 2025, the company is off to another great start in 2026, even as fears around an AI bubble grow.

Tempus AI is one of the leading AI-focused companies in the healthcare industry. Adoption of AI technologies remains nascent, however, the company's propriety data of personalized healthcare treatment suggestions is gaining traction.

As seen in the chart above, the last few months of 2025 brought heightened scrutiny around AI valuations. Volatility in TEM shares is not unusual, and these periods of volatility often present great buying opportunities. I have long been bullish on the company, and my bullish thesis remains the same.

For Tempus AI, I believe the buying opportunity right now is clear.

Two key drivers behind the "buy now" thesis include recent positive commentary from the J.P. Morgan Conference and the general improvement in AI adoption. More on both of those below.

Financial Update

Although Q4 financials have not been officially reported, Tempus AI provided preliminary results during the J.P. Morgan Conference earlier this month. This conference is often viewed as the golden standard for Healthcare conferences, and I believe Tempus AI's showing is being underappreciated.

Given some of the market fears around AI hype, I believe it was a very smart, proactive decision for Tempus AI to provide preliminary Q4 results.

In fact, these results were stronger than some might have expected, with the company seeing a record >$1.1 billion in total contract value.

For the full-year, preliminary revenue is expected to be ~$1.27 billion, similar to the prior guidance of $1.265 billion, and representing ~30% organic growth. Although not directly disclosed, management cited "positive adjusted EBITDA" for the full-year, which was also similar to guidance.

With Tempus AI now having >$1.1 billion in TCV, this should give investors additional comfort in the growth runway over the next few years. Management provided some commentary on the TCV trends, underscoring its importance and how this will help generate profitable growth in future periods.

We announced last night that we have over $1.1 billion of total contract value in place. So this essentially is the -- I think most people think of this like a bookings metric. It's the total value of the data licenses we have under contract to deliver in future periods. There's a small amount of this that's opt-ins, but the majority of it is just contracted revenue where people have -- they're just licensing our data and have to fulfill their contract terms. We don't include milestones or bio bucks or any weird number in this. This is just real money, real cash.

As a reminder, TCV is just a snapshot of the financial performance at the end of the quarter. This does not represent all of the revenue that will be recognized, but rather, just the already-signed deals. For example, in 2025, Tempus AI signed and delivered $100 million of revenue that was recorded in-revenue. Given the company's revenue base of $1.27 billion, this represented nearly 10% of total revenue. With the company still experience strong momentum, investors should expect another strong year of in-period signed and delivered deals.

Tempus AI also provided 2026 guidance, which included revenue growing 25% yoy to $1.59 billion and adjusted EBITDA expanding to ~$65 million. Compared to the 2025 organic revenue growth of ~30%, this does imply a few points of deceleration, but this should be expected as the company continues to scale.

What's more impressive to me is the pace of adjusted EBITDA expansion. Essentially breakeven in 2025, achieving $65 million would be a nice jump. On an absolute dollar basis, revenue is expected to grow ~$320 million and adjusted EBITDA is expected to grow ~$65 million, representing an incremental adjusted EBITDA margin of ~20%. This margin expansion also includes the company's decision to "reinvest two-thirds of incremental growth profit dollars back into the business."

In my opinion, this demonstrates the company's optimistic view around the growth opportunity as well as how profitable the business can become over time.

Growing Platform And Adoption

Like many industries, healthcare is still in the very early stages of adopting AI technology. Given the security protocols needed to protect sensitive patient data, the healthcare industry is likely to adopt AI at a steady pace.

In fact, one could argue that Tempus AI's approach is superior. Rather than being a horizontal provider of data -- in other words, provide solutions to multiple industries -- Tempus AI's focus on healthcare can give them a competitive advantage. Vertical AI companies typically have more domain expertise, giving customers higher levels of confidence.

The long-term opportunity remains very large, as many healthcare processes are manual and mundane. As Tempus AI builds out its product portfolio and continues to win deals, traction should only build.

As seen in the chart above, Tempus AI has spent several years building out its product portfolio across multiple disease areas. With the company's products achieving high efficacy rates, this can turn into a positive virtuous circle.

Management recently talked about the success they are seeing with the data.

And as a result, if you look at like last year, I don't think I ever heard any one of our sales reps or leadership come to us -- come to me at any point in time ever and say, we didn't get a data deal, somebody else got it. Zero. So that's extraordinary. And I don't think great. I would much prefer they had better data businesses so we could collectively educate the market on how valuable this data is. But at the present moment, probably because we're a tech company at heart and a diagnostic company second, we've invested enormous amounts of money north -- well north of $1 billion in amassing data and building tools that make that data useful.

As mentioned above, the >$1.1 billion in TCV is another indicator of growing AI adoption. The strong multi-year book of business is likely to grow from the Q4 levels, thus providing a strong base for revenue growth.

In addition to TCV rising to the highest level in Tempus’s history, the company reported net revenue retention of ~126% in 2025, demonstrating the continued expansion of relationships with existing customers, even at increased scale.

With ~126% net revenue retention, according to the company, this is another proof points that existing customers are coming back and spending more with Tempus AI. In a traditional software business, ~126% NRR would be considered top-class. For Tempus AI, I believe this is a demonstration of the company's product investments, acquisition history, and willingness on the behalf of customers to spend more on AI.

Valuation

As with many AI focused companies, one of the biggest pushbacks is often around valuation. Part of investing into emerging technologies is embedding some sort of "hype" premium into shares. Essentially, this is the investor confidence in the company's technology being able to translate into scaled, profitable revenue. For Tempus AI, the company has clearly shown their ability to grow organically and start to generate meaningful amounts of revenue.

Valuation now sits at ~7.6x forward revenue. At face value, many investors would consider this to be expensive. However, this is a far cry away from the high point of >17x forward revenue in late-2024.

One thing to consider is the growth trajectory of the business. Management just provided 2026 revenue growth of ~25% on top of material adjusted EBITDA growth. In a normalized environment - one that is not plagued by mixed AI sentiment - a company with these financial metrics would be trading closer to 10x forward revenue.

So yes, ~7.6x forward revenue might not screen cheap, but one should consider the multi-year growth opportunity here. If growth can remain 20%+ for the next few years, which appears likely, then current valuation presents a great buying opportunity.

Risks

One of the biggest areas of risk is around deliverability and future contracts wins. Yes, the >$1.1 billion in TCV is a great number, but Tempus AI still needs to deliver on these contracts. If issues were to arise with their products, this could cause significant contract cancellations. Although a low probability event, this would have a material impact on the business.

Additionally, as Tempus AI continues to prove out its market fit, this could attract the attention of competitors. AI is a fast-evolving market, and increased competition could make new wins more challenging and potentially result in price concessions.

Final Thoughts

With the stock still down over 30% from its October 2025 highs, this pullback presents an attractive buying opportunity. Tempus AI recently provided preliminary Q4 results that demonstrated strong execution and customer traction. The inclusion of 2026 guidance metrics should give investors increased confidence in the growth trajectory.

Tempus AI has carved out a niche position in the market and I believe the company will grow into one of the leading healthcare AI companies.

I believe the stock will continue to trade with high levels of volatility, which can make building a position challenge. Long-term investors should look to take advantage of future dips.

data.title: Netflix: One More Downside Catalyst Ahead (Hold Until April)
data.description:
Right after the Warner Bros. Discovery acquisition announcement, I rated Netflix, Inc. (NFLX) as a sell. Overall, I wasn't excited by the high financing costs and the regulatory uncertainty that this deal would bring.

Since then, the story has shifted to whether margins can stay intact while the deal is funded. In the Q4 earnings release, management guided 31.5% in operating margins. That represents a margin expansion compared to FY 2025, despite the $275M headwind due to the Warner Bros. Discovery financing acquisition costs.

Overall, valuation is more compelling at 27x forward earnings, and from a technical point of view, shares look quite attractive. That's why I upgraded to a hold.

However, I see a downside catalyst ahead. Heading into WBD's shareholder decision in April, I believe Netflix could be forced to match Paramount’s $30/share offer if it wants to win the bid. In my view, this could increase bridge financing and pressure the 2026 margin expectations. A downgrade from the 31.5% operating margin could hurt the stock.

Overall, I prefer to stay on the sidelines until we have more clarification on the WBD bid.

The Market Is Not Excited About The Warner Bros. Acquisition, why?

From a technical perspective, the December 5 announcement to acquire Warner Bros. couldn’t have been timed worse.

After the $619M Brazil tax dispute in Q3, the downward momentum picked up. Since shares broke the long term uptrend (right after the WBD announcement), the stock has been on a freefall.

In theory, the market should welcome the Warner Bros. deal. Netflix said it targets $2–3B of annual cost savings by year 3 and expects the transaction to be accretive to GAAP EPS by year 2. From a timeline perspective, the close was guided as 12–18 months from the December 2025 announcement, so the tailwind to EPS could come "as soon" as 2029.

You see the problem, right?

The concern is what happens to fundamentals until then. The main issue, assuming the acquisition goes through, seems to be financing and, therefore, margins.

The deal was valued at $82.7B in enterprise value and $72.0B in equity value. Initially, Netflix offered $23.25 in cash and $4.50 in stock. However, after the hostile bid from Paramount ($30 per share in cash) and the selloff in Netflix stock, the company updated its bid to $27.75 in an all-cash transaction.

The headwinds from acquisition-related costs are expected to be around $275 million in 2026 (which have been embedded in management's 31.5% operating margin guidance). On top of that $275M, don't forget the $60M of financing related costs already recognized in Q4.

Speaking of financing, the $59B bridge commitment disclosed in December came down to $34B after a $5B revolving credit facility and a $20B delayed-draw term loan were approved.

However, given the recent change to an all-cash transaction, they added $8.2B in bridge commitments, now totaling $42.2B.

I am not particularly excited about the size of the bridge commitments compared to the deal's $72B equity value.

First, bridge facilities have a shorter term duration and are typically more expensive than permanent bond financing. So far, 59% of the deal is financed through bridge commitments. However, the risk I see is that WBD shareholders could ask Netflix to match Paramount’s $30/share bid. If that happens after the WBD shareholder vote (expected by April 2026), I think Netflix will need to increase its bridge facilities again. That would likely pressure operating margins, since the $275M in acquisition-related costs in 2026 would probably tick up. Therefore, this is the biggest risk to consider in the near term.

Looking at WBD's stock price, it seems that the market is divided between Paramount's offer ($30) and Netflix's ($27.5).

Prediction markets believe Netflix is the top contender to win the bid, although I believe they will need to match Paramount's offer for this to happen.

Lastly, regulation.

According to the latest update, Netflix and WBD submitted the Hart-Scott-Rodino filings and are currently engaging with the U.S. Department of Justice and the European Commission. To be honest, I don’t think either Netflix or Paramount would have bid for WBD if they weren’t confident an acquisition would be possible. However, the risk here is mainly about the timeline to close the deal.

If any extended reviews are needed, the 12-18 month timeline could be delayed even more, which will likely pressure the stock. Never underestimate the shadow that uncertainty can cast over a stock.

Earnings Results Were Actually Decent

The company reported a top-line growth in FY 2025 of 16%. Looking at the quarterly growth for the past couple of years, Q4 revenue growth was a record high (17.6%).

A good part of this growth is driven by the ads business. Netflix reported that 2025 ad revenue rose 2.5x to over $1.5B. Looking ahead, the CFO expects to roughly double the company's ad revenue in 2026:

So on the revenue side, for 2026, key drivers are similar to '25. So it's membership growth, it's pricing, and it's a rough doubling of our ad revenue in 2026 to about $3 billion

From a revenue guidance perspective, management expects 2026 revenue to be $50.7B–$51.7B (representing a growth rate between 12%-14% yoy). Not bad, although this represents a slowdown compared to FY2026.

Operating margins were 29.5% for the year, up 3 pts yoy. However, Q4 saw a significant sequential decline to 24.5% from 28.2% in Q3. I believe part of this decline is due to the $60M in financing costs related to the WBD acquisition during the quarter.

A (highly anticipated) disappointment was the pause in buybacks due to the WBD acquisition. By the end of Q4, the buyback program had $8.0B left under authorization.

Looking ahead, the company expects an operating margin of 31.5% in 2026. This would represent an increase to the FY25 margin, despite the WBD financing costs.

As I noted above, I strongly believe this guidance may be at risk. If Netflix upgrades its bid to Paramount's $30/share, I think the $275M in acquisition-related expenses (baked into the 2026 guidance) will increase, likely due to an increase in bridge commitments.

Valuation

After the selloff since the highs in July last year, the stock is getting cheap. On a forward P/E basis, Netflix is trading at 27x.

Is that cheap? Well, a quick look at the table below, representing a comparison with other streaming video subscription services, suggests that Netflix is indeed attractively priced.

On a forward P/E basis, the company is the second cheapest in the group, after Disney.

Conclusion

I would stay on the sidelines for now. At least, until the WBD shareholder decision in April. In my view, the stock has one more negative catalyst ahead if Netflix matches Paramount's bid offer of $30/share. If this happens, I believe the operating margin guidance for FY2026 could see a haircut due to higher financing expenses. So far, management expects $275M in financing costs due to the acquisition. If they hike their bid to $30/share, I think the bridge commitments could increase from the current $42.2B.

That said, I would closely monitor this stock for any dips after an upgraded bid offer. Excluding the uncertainty around the WBD acquisition, fundamentals are strong, and I think valuation makes sense after the 35% selloff since the highs in July. Until then, I rate Netflix as a hold.

data.title: Intel: Government And Nvidia Backings Not Enough, Shares Materially Overpriced
data.description:
The U.S. government (and its taxpayers) is sitting pretty on its Intel Corporation (INTC) stake. With a cost basis down near $20 per share, the once-left-for-dead legacy semiconductor industry company has seen tremendous growth in the last four-plus months. With financial backing from the Trump administration, there’s arguably an embedded put option—if shares dip significantly, the government could step up and purchase more shares.

But that’s not a sufficient Buy thesis in my view. The fundamentals still matter. Following Q4 results, I am issuing a Sell rating on Intel's common equity. The debt actually looks in a strong position given the balance sheet improvement, but shares are simply overvalued based on where earnings may be. What’s more, I see technical downside into the low $40s, which I will detail at the conclusion of the article.

INTC Big Gains Last 4 Months

In January, Intel reported a solid set of quarterly results. Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $0.15 topped the Wall Street consensus forecast of $0.08, while revenue of $13.7 billion, down 4% from the same quarter a year earlier, was a $310 million beat.

But the stock traded lower by 12% by Friday morning on the heels of a soft outlook. The management team issued Q1 2026 revenue guidance of $11.7 billion to $12.7 billion, with Q1 EPS attributable to Intel of -$0.21 and non-GAAP EPS attributable to Intel of $0.00.

CEO Lip-Bu Tan noted that there were manufacturing problems in the quarter. Now 1/17th the size of Nvidia (NVDA), it remains a small portion of the chip market, with a slew of CEOs who have tried to turn around the firm. But keep in mind that Nvidia did take a $5 billion stake late last year.

Shares plunged on the Friday after earnings, pacing for the worst post-reporting performance since August 2024. The options market had priced in a large 9% stock price swing, with implied volatility very high at 68% before the numbers hit. INTC has now traded lower after earnings in seven of the last nine instances. Shares of the $250 billion market cap Information Technology sector stock do not have a trailing 12-month dividend, while short interest is low but not nothing, 2.33%.

Looking back on the quarter that was, the top and bottom line beats were overshadowed by light guidance. What’s more, investors were likely disappointed by low internal yields and shortages of semiconductor substrates, DRAM, and NAND, which are constraining PC production despite strong data center demand. Intel appears to be prioritizing supply to data centers, but it cannot meet full PC market demand. Those shortages could hamper growth, I fear.

Q4 Numbers

Still, Q4 marked a step forward in the firm’s transition. Its non-GAAP gross margin hit 37.9%, but that was down 4.2 percentage points from the same quarter of 2024. Adjusted free cash flow improved to $2.2 billion, supported by government incentives and partner contributions.

Operationally, the company built a strong foundation in 2025 by strengthening its balance sheet, right-sizing the organization, and advancing key products. It ramped its Core Ultra Series 3 in its Client Computing Group and launched nodes in Intel Foundry.

On the call, the management team relayed to the street that it would take “time and resolve” for the full turnaround and transition to materialize in larger EPS. So, it was a bit of a reality check after the stunning stock price rally.

Q1 Forecast

On the earnings outlook, EPS growth is expected to be strong in FY 2026 with intense growth in the out year. But FY 2028, the firm could approach $1.50 in non-GAAP EPS. I still believe it’s a show-me story, particularly now given the light forecast from within the Q4 report and call.

The sell-side, meanwhile, has been generally upbeat about INTC, with 12 earnings upgrades in the past 90 days compared to just seven downgrades. Finally, profitability trends are healthy.

Intel: Revenue & Earnings Outlook, EPS Revision Trends

On valuation, if we assume normalized forward operating EPS of $1.06 (the FY 2027 consensus) and apply a generous 30x multiple, then shares should trade near $32. That makes shares overvalued.

Intel: Elevated Valuation Multiples, High P/S

Key risks include lower yields with its Foundry business, slower customer growth in wafer processing, weak PC sales, disruption in the data center growth area, and, of course, an unstable Trump administration. The global supply chain and economic growth trends are other areas of potential uncertainty.

Competitor Analysis

Looking ahead, corporate event data provided by Wall Street Horizon show an unconfirmed Q1 2026 earnings date of Thursday, April 23 AMC. No other volatility catalysts are seen on the calendar.

Corporate Event Risk Calendar

The Technical Take

With shares materially overvalued in my view, INTC’s technical situation is mixed. Notice in the chart below that shares paused near the December 2023 peak and the high from early 2022. I see support down in the low to mid-$40s. I expect the stock to trade down to that zone in the coming weeks or months after the Q1 report and reaction.

But take a look at the long-term 200-day moving average. It’s on the rise, suggesting that the bulls now control the primary trend. What’s more, the RSI momentum oscillator at the top of the graph has been in a bullish range since last summer. Ultimately, the upside case is that INTC eventually tests the $68 to $70 area, but some digestion here is certainly warranted.

INTC: Shares Hit Resistance, $43 Support

The Bottom Line

I have a Sell rating on INTC. I see shares as materially overvalued, while the chart points to a pullback toward $43.

data.title: Canada Goose: Growth Investments Need To Pay Off
data.description:
Canada Goose Holdings Inc. (GOOS) is going to report the company’s fiscal Q3 results from the October-December period on the 5th of February. The quarter is especially important to Canada Goose given the winter outerwear brand’s seasonality; a lot of focus is on the upcoming report. Previous store and marketing investments need to materialize as sales growth after earnings pressure in H1.

I maintained a Hold rating in my previous April 2025 article on the stock, titled “Canada Goose: Performance Is Far From Investor Day Ambition”. The stock has since returned 55%, outperforming the S&P 500’s 31% gain.

Let’s See If Canada Goose's Growth Investments Will Pay Off in Q3

The upcoming fiscal Q3 report will be critical for Canada Goose. As a brand that’s primarily focused on winter outerwear, Canada Goose generates the majority of its revenue during winter months. The season also naturally comes with higher margins as a result of sharply higher sales and better margins on winter products; Canada Goose typically offsets its first-half losses with great earnings in the second half, especially in Q3. Canada Goose’s recent investments make achieving growth important.

Revenues are expected by Wall Street to come in at around CAD 658 million, up by 8.2% year-on-year. The adjusted EPS is expected at CAD 1.64, up by 8.9% year-on-year. Consensus estimates suggest good revenue growth, accompanied by a stable margin performance.

The report will work as a measure of Canada Goose’s product and marketing strategies’ success. Canada Goose’s fall-winter 2025 collection tries to capture a more urban look in its aesthetics while still catering to the brand’s core high-performance outdoors market; the collection differs noticeably from previous product curation.

On the marketing front, Canada Goose has invested in brand storytelling, including signing Canadian NBA champion Shai Gilgeous-Alexander and Taiwanese actor Hsu Kuang-han as brand ambassadors. Canada Goose increased its marketing spend by CAD 10.5 million in Q2 while shifting the marketing budget towards upper funnel activity; the company is clearly investing in its brand image to create long-term demand and brand relevance. A shift into upper funnel marketing could moderate purchase conversions over the short term, but still be an effective longer-term strategy to maintain brand relevance.

Another key focus point is Canada Goose’s direct-to-consumer performance. Investments in store openings have driven Canada Goose’s sales mix towards the higher-margin channel, and a more consistent retail execution has driven very strong purchase conversions in stores. Canada Goose’s previous Q2 report included rapid 10.2% comparable store sales growth, being the third consecutive quarter with impressive growth in the metric. Positive direct-to-consumer momentum was maintained in October according to Q2 earnings call remarks, but the Q3 report will most importantly measure the channel’s continuation in the seasonally critical November-December period. As DTC growth has been partially driven by Canada Goose’s product diversification into year-round categories, I don’t assume the winter quarter to show as good growth in comparable sales anymore.

Regardless, previous momentum sets up Canada Goose for a successful Q3. Measuring brand relevance by Canada Goose’s Google search volume, the company should report good revenue growth in the quarter. Canada Goose showed very good year-on-year gains from mid-October to late November, afterwards showing a consistent year-on-year performance in the metric. The metric’s short-term signaling power is limited, though; Canada Goose’s final Q3 results will be a more robust measure of brand relevance.

Higher marketing expenses and other store growth investments have caused a $27.1 million year-on-year decline in adjusted EBIT during the first half – growth investments need to pay off in the winter quarter. Wall Street assumes profitability to stabilize year-on-year in Q3, and stabilization clearly requires higher revenue to offset a higher SG&A level.

Luxury Sector Sluggishness May Weigh on Canada Goose

More negatively, the luxury apparel sector has still been in a drought. Weak Chinese consumer spending and consumers’ overall shift away from luxury apparel have weighed on the sector globally. Based on data that I have collected, the calendar Q3 (Canada Goose’s fiscal Q2) period showed a -4.8% overall decrease in reported luxury fashion revenue across publicly traded sector companies; Canada Goose is swimming against the tide in the market.

While JPMorgan already sees some positive developments in the luxury market outlook, the fourth quarter doesn’t seem to have a very strong overall outlook yet. Canada Goose’s growth has to come from market share gains in the upcoming report, being a noteworthy headwind. Growth investments need to more than offset industry weakness.

There’s Some Upside in GOOS Stock

I updated my discounted cash flow (DCF) model for Canada Goose. I estimate slightly better growth in the next few years than previously, aided by Canada Goose’s solid DTC growth and marketing investments. The FY2025-FY2035 CAGR is still set at an overall moderate 3.3% level, assuming growth to slow down to a 2% perpetual rate.

I expect Canada Goose’s adjusted EBIT margin to stabilize at 12.5%, suggesting some improvement from the current margin performance. Growth investments have weighed on short-term results, but should create the foundation for better longer-term earnings. The cash flow conversion outlook is fairly good after taxes.

The estimates put Canada Goose’s fair value at $16.0, 27% above the stock price as of writing. There’s some upside in the investment case, but for now, the upside isn’t enough to suggest a Buy rating in my opinion. Risks in the overall luxury fashion market, and in Canada Goose’s reported growth rate have to be noted.

The fair value estimate is up from $12.5 previously.

CAPM

The DCF model uses a cost of capital [WACC] of 8.65%, derived from a capital asset pricing model:

I estimate an 8.42% interest rate, annualized from Q2 interest expenses and debt. I again expect a fairly high 30% long-term debt-to-equity ratio.

For cost of equity, I use Canada’s 10-year bond yield of 3.41% as the risk-free rate. The equity risk premium of 4.23% is Professor Aswath Damodaran’s most recent January estimate for Canada. I have kept the beta estimate at 1.33. With a liquidity premium of 0.3%, the cost of equity stands at 9.24% and the WACC at 8.65%.

Takeaway

Canada Goose is facing a challenging market backdrop in the seasonally critical fiscal Q3 report. Regardless, the market and I both expect good growth – previous investments in store expansion and marketing should start to pay off depending on the fall-winter 2025 collection’s success. Significant growth investments, causing earnings pressure in previous quarters, makes an improved topline critical as Wall Street expects Canada Goose’s margins to stabilize through sales growth.

There’s some upside in GOOS stock’s valuation, but due to current risks, I cautiously remain at a Hold rating.

data.title: Will U.S. Q4 Growth Exceed Q3's Strong Pace?
data.description:
Economic activity grew at a strong pace in the second and third quarters, according to official GDP data published by the Bureau of Economic Analysis. The party continued in Q4, based on a widely followed nowcast that projects that the robust expansion in the two previous quarters will accelerate in the government’s delayed GDP report due next month.

The Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow model is estimating that the economy increased 5.4% at an annualized rate in Q4 (as of Jan. 22). If correct, the estimate will blow past the strong 4.4% rise in Q3 and mark the strongest quarterly increase in four years.

As always, there are a number of caveats to consider in the realm of nowcasting. For starters, the GDPNow estimate has yet to show up in other estimates monitored by CapitalSpectator.com. The Atlanta Fed’s nowcast is the outlier, by a wide margin. That doesn’t mean it’s wrong. But until other nowcasts move closer to the GDPNow estimate, a degree of caution is recommended in expecting Q4 growth to accelerate.

Using the median nowcast from several models highlights a considerably slower Q4 growth rate: 2.1%. That’s still a respectable gain, but it’s a long way from 5-plus%.

On firmer ground, it’s becoming clear that the odds are low, if not nil, that a US recession started in Q4. Instead, the debate about next month’s delayed release of GDP data for the October-through-December period is whether the expansion is downshifting or picking up.

The analysis is (still) unusually tricky at the moment due to lingering effects on the economic data collection from the government shutdown in October.

Samuel Tombs, chief US economist at Pantheon Macroeconomics is among the skeptics questioning the sizzling GDPNow’s Q4 estimate, which he says is “highly questionable.” The consultancy estimates Q4 growth is substantially softer, in the 1.5% range. A key reason for doubt: some of the data during the previous quarter is still missing, and some of what has been published is questionable.

Nonetheless, a broad review of economic activity across a variety of metrics strongly suggests that the US continued to grow at a pace that minimizes recession risk. Confidently estimating the pace of growth, by contrast, is still a work in progress.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Kratos Defense: Stretched Valuation But Options Offer Attractive Returns
data.description:
Background

Kratos Defense & Security Solutions, Inc. (KTOS) is an American technology company focused on the defense and military sectors.

The company was founded in 1994 as Wireless Facilities Incorporated, providing support for telecommunications infrastructure. In 2007, the company changed its name to Kratos Defense & Security Solutions as the company divested its commercial wireless businesses and shifted its focus to defense. Initially, it was a subcontractor, but it has evolved into a global defense leader with a key characteristic being its rapid deployment of new technologies.

Now, the company is divided into six different divisions covering most of the possible defense needs with more than 30 different programs.

The company began to stand out with its Full-Scale Aerial Target, which was the foundation of its more advanced tactical and combat UAS, like the XQ-58 Valkyrie.

Since then, the company has invested in different products and segments, with its Hypersonic franchise expected to be the most important growth driver in the future. In fact, during the last conference call, the CEO commented:

Kratos is the world's technological leader in developing, building, and flying affordable hypersonic systems, including our first-to-market hypersonic flyers, Erinyes and Dark Fury, our first-to-market Zeus 1 and Zeus 2 solid rocket motors, and certain other hypersonic systems we have now successfully flown.

We expect Kratos' hypersonic franchise, including the MACH-TB program and additional programs we have, including one with our partner, Lockheed Martin, to be an important future growth driver for Kratos beginning in 2026.

Kratos is making significant investments in several facilities to achieve the expected growth, which is expected to come online in 2027 and 2028. This is the main reason the company does not distribute cash to shareholders, as it is investing all its free cash flow back in the business.

Financial Position and Valuation

Kratos has a strong financial position with $566 million in cash and no debt. This was achieved through an equity offering in June 2025, where the company raised $575 million.

All cash generated over the last few years, plus the proceeds of the equity offering, will be invested in increasing production capacity and developing new products. This is why the company could become one of the relevant defense players in a few years.

In the last conference call, management commented that they expect growth to accelerate:

And we are providing today a preliminary 2027 revenue growth target of 18% to 23% organic growth above the 2026 revenue range, which is 15% to 20% above 2025.

And this is before the full impact of the hypersonic growth. Taking this into consideration, I believe that the company could reach $5 billion in revenue in 2030. This will justify the elevated current valuation; however, there are important execution risks that have to be considered.

Assuming the $5 billion in revenue and applying a 4x P/S ratio, due to its higher growth compared to consolidated peers like LHX and RTX, this will justify a $20 billion valuation, in line with the current market cap. However, since those are 2030 revenues, they should be discounted. I believe its current fair value should be around $16 billion, implying a $90 share price, which is below the current price.

This is why I’m not long the stock at current levels; however, because of the elevated implied volatility, I tend to sell puts at prices where I’m interested in owning the stock. For example, March 90 puts could be sold at $4.1.

Risks

Kratos’ main risk is related to execution and scaling. Due to its elevated growth and investment in new facilities and capacity, successfully scaling manufacturing, supply chains, and workforce could be difficult. Delays or cost overruns could compress margins and delay deliveries.

Another risk is that a significant portion of its contracts are fixed price. If costs rise, the company could face margin pressures.

In addition, the stock is highly volatile, and because of the elevated valuation, it could drop without any news.

Conclusion

Kratos is set to become one of the leading defense companies, with a wide range of products.

While I consider the current valuation somewhat stretched, my fair value is $90 per share. Given the positive geopolitical environment and my confidence in the company, I want to be exposed to its upside. I have opted to sell OTM puts to stay involved with the company while reducing the downside.

data.title: Applied Materials: When Chip Complexity Becomes The Real Alpha
data.description:
Thesis

Even after a 67% gain in Applied Materials (AMAT) since my last article, it continues to be a "Strong Buy". We all know that much of the economy is being driven by AI and therefore by capital expenditures, supply chain, and valuation. However, the question is somewhat different from simply wondering if AI will grow; rather, we're trying to determine which companies profit most as chip manufacturing becomes increasingly complex. I believe that markets are becoming far less "democratic". Not all AI will rise. Not all will profit from AI as there will be a ruthless selection process.

When complexity increases, not everyone profits equally. AMAT, yes.

I believe 2026 may be a nervous year with a great deal of volatility. My strategy for AMAT is straightforward. I have the stock in my portfolio and do not want to purchase near all-time highs. Therefore, I am not "chasing" the stock. I am waiting. However, my wait is short, and I am only waiting until I can enter the stock in a more "aesthetically pleasing manner". The top area to purchase AMAT for me is between $278 and $288. For the stock, I have two clear levels: target one ($352) and target two ($381).

As mentioned before, I won’t go into detail on how Applied Materials sells to whom or where, but you’ll find answers to those questions in the company overview section of my prior analysis. Additionally, if you would like to know more, please feel free to comment below and I’ll add additional information. As stated earlier, this is an update on the stock.

Monetizing Complexity

As previously mentioned, something has changed. Perhaps it’s more related to the sentiment. Following multiple meetings with players in the semiconductor industry, KeyBanc reaffirmed their Overweight rating for Applied Materials (along with other semiconductor stocks) and raised their price target from $285 to $380. Let’s discuss the reasoning behind the upgrades, however. Analysts believe that the building of next-generation chips is becoming increasingly difficult to create. The new generation of chips requires more steps in the manufacturing process, more layers of material to be deposited onto the chip, and therefore, more etching. The more complicated the manufacturing process, the greater the complexity. AMAT makes a large share of its profit from each of these steps, since they sell the equipment used during each of the steps and continue to generate revenue through the entire manufacturing process. Therefore, the more complicated the manufacturing process, the higher the margin.

I want to take a moment to address what many may consider to be a less publicly known segment of Applied Materials' business model, which, although less well-known than some of Applied Materials’ other segments, is vital to the success of the company. That segment is Applied Global Services (AGS).

To some, it may appear to be the most “boring” aspect of the business, if you will. However, it is by far the most stable. While the manufacturing facilities are operating at peak levels, the equipment used in the facility needs to be maintained, updated, and repaired. In turn, that is where AGS generates significant revenue almost automatically. Not coincidentally, it was identified as one of the primary sources of revenue for Applied Materials for the upcoming years.

The question of why AGS is generating revenue so easily, is directly related to the increasing complexity of the chips. As the complexity of the chips increases, the manufacturing facilities cannot afford to shut down the equipment. The equipment used in these facilities costs hundreds of millions of dollars, runs continuously 24/7 and every minute of downtime equates to lost revenue.

More complex processes place greater demands on the equipment, and require additional maintenance, additional upgrade efforts, and additional intervention efforts. In addition, when Applied Materials sells a chip-making machine, it does not simply sell one time - it also sells ongoing maintenance (i.e., service), spare parts, product updates, and optimization services. Every year. Therefore, the revenue stream from Applied Materials is based not on the total number of chips manufactured by its customers, but on the intensity with which the customers use their chip-making machines. And as I mentioned earlier, the customers are using them very heavily right now.

Therefore, analysts are increasing their optimism about Applied Materials' stock price not because the cycle is back again, but rather because they believe that there will be a structural shift in the way in which Applied Materials' customers produce chips, and that Applied Materials is very well-positioned for this shift in customer demand.

So, the context in which we are looking at 2026, is going to matter a lot. Capex spending is going to be anything but normal in 2026. The cloud players continue to be "big spenders", and the supply chain is tightening up, particularly with regard to memory. This is not an encouraging trend line, however, as is so often the case in times of tight supply chains and high capex spending, the best investment opportunities appear.

Therefore, in this environment, what is most important is maximizing the revenue generated by the complexity of the manufacturing process (i.e., generating revenue from each wafer) versus the pure quantity of wafers manufactured (i.e., the number of chips produced).

That's my interpretation. It is not a certainty; it is a reasonable wager. And for me, today, it represents a compelling rationale.

Earnings

When it comes to the quarterly results, I recommend going through the specific section of the Seeking Alpha that provides an excellent summary of the earnings season. Below is my take (as well as some other things I am interested in), but it does not provide every detail, as I do not focus on "beats" or "misses." What is far more interesting is the type of year we are having.

Taken collectively, the numbers paint a fairly straightforward picture. Applied Materials exceeded earnings expectations in all four quarterly reports in FY25. There were no fireworks, however, there was solid execution throughout the year. Linear. Orderly.

The estimates for FY26 create a classic two part year: three soft quarters followed by a very strong fourth quarter. While this pattern is common, it is less common than it seems upon initial review. The reason for this is based on the quarterly drivers and is entirely technical. For the first three quarters, revenues are estimated to fall slightly (year-over-year), while gross margin is estimated to fall slightly. As a result, operating profit is falling more quickly than revenue, creating unfavorable operating leverage and resulting in negative EPS growth for the first three quarters.

The story changes radically in the fourth quarter, when revenue is still expected to show a substantial increase of 15.5% year-over-year compared to last year, with margins also improving. Operating profit is estimated to increase by approximately 25.5% in the fourth quarter, leading to an estimated 26% increase in earnings per share (EPS), as you can see in the image below from the "Earnings" section of Seeking Alpha.

The important thing to know and understand is that these estimates are already priced into the stock, so any slight improvement (a slightly better-than-expected performance in one of the quarters, or a slightly better performance in the recovery of growth) will translate into a positive market reaction. While the market historically reacts poorly to three consecutive quarters of declining EPS, I believe this situation should be viewed as if the increase in EPS has simply been delayed.

In addition to the fact that negative Qs are already reflected in price, I'd like to highlight that both the CEO & CFO have stated that they expect the majority of 2026's growth to come from the 2nd half of the year. They've emphasized this due to customer launch schedules, which are currently expected to occur in the 2nd half of the year. The CEO/CFO has continually emphasized an improvement in customer visibility over the next 1-2 years, specifically in the foundry/advanced logic and DRAM markets. This is important because it mitigates the risk that the accelerated 4Q is simply a model-driven event.

Management expects all of their products (including those in the advanced logic, DRAM, and high-bandwidth memory) to grow faster than average in 2026, and these product families are exactly where Applied has its best competitive position and highest market share.

Additionally, the buyback program is interesting as Applied paid back $6.3B in dividends/share repurchases in 2025, and in doing so increased its dividend payout by 15%, and reduced its common shares outstanding by greater than 3% through $4.9B in share repurchases. Share buybacks will automatically add a multiplier effect to the recovery when Applied's operating profit returns to growth.

Valuation

Seeking Alpha’s valuation tool gives us a “F” and if we only looked at the tool, we would probably not buy shares of AMAT. The tool’s valuation is correct since the multiples are higher than the industry averages: the non-GAAP P/E ratio is approximately 34x vs the median of around 25x; and the EV/EBITDA is above 28x compared to 19x.

The multiple does reflect an FY26 that has started off slowly, so the current valuation is punishing the timing and not the long-term growth of the business. The market is already building into the next several years of earnings growth, that is estimated to occur after the end of FY26 and into FY27. As long as that curve remains intact, the valuation may seem stretched without being irrational. The only risk is that the acceleration of earnings that the company is projecting, will not occur. At that point, the multiple will begin to deteriorate.

When we compare AMAT’s valuation to its peers, the picture looks less severe than it might initially appear. Currently, AMAT’s BF P/E ratio is approximately 31-32x. As the following chart from Bloomberg shows, the overall P/E ratio of the peer group is significantly higher at approximately 36-37x.

This analysis is also fairly similar when we look at the forward EV/EBITDA multiple. Clearly, AMAT is at the high-end of the spectrum for the sector, but it is relatively consistent with the large-cap peers in the sector.

The consensus is also aligned; in fact, the consensus rating is 4.26 with approximately 67% of the analysts suggesting a "buy" recommendation.

My strategy remains the same as I mentioned before; therefore, I'll accumulate shares of the company only in the $278-$288 price range, where I believe the risks associated with the purchase are better for me. Should the earnings growth acceleration occur from the end of FY26 through FY27 (i.e., should both revenue and margin work in our favor), the normal revaluation will initially take the stock to $352 (Target 1) and subsequently to $381 (Target 2), which are also the extensions of the Fibonacci projections.

Risks

The biggest risks to the company's valuation and its future share price relate to both the valuation process (i.e., how much investors will pay for each dollar of earnings) and to the actual level of profitability.

If the growth rate in the company's profits does not grow at least as quickly as investors expect (in this case, from the end of fiscal year '26 to the end of fiscal year '27), then the company will likely experience an additional decline in its price relative to its earnings because of multiple contraction. A decline in profitability could also occur if there were problems with capital expenditures (capex). While long-term demand for the products produced by the company is strong, clients do not invest in capex on a linear basis.

Finally, investor expectations for the company have grown significantly. Therefore, investors may be reluctant to continue to bid up the value of their shares until they feel that the company's prospects have been reaffirmed through a series of positive events.

Conclusion

As I said in the previous section on valuation and risk, there could be a lot of turmoil with earnings per share (EPS) declining in the first three quarters of 2026, but at least it has been priced into the stock price. So, do not worry about it for now. Valuations are at high levels, and the market is going to be persistent again, particularly this year in my view. The market will be less democratic than it was previously, and will penalize stocks much more severely for lackluster results.

Looking at the long term, we see a company that monetizes throughout the entire cycle thanks to the various services it offers.​‍​‌‍​‍‌ It is possible that earnings acceleration will arrive later or may not be as clean as expected. ​‍​‌‍​‍‌This is the risk of the thesis; there's no point in beating around the bush. But as long as the trajectory remains stable, weakness for me is more of an opportunity than a warning sign.

data.title: NVE Corporation: Reliable Sensor Business, Unreliable Dividend Math
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The common wisdom is that analog semiconductor companies stand to benefit from the rise in robotics demand (in factories and, more recently, humanoids and consumer robotics). ABI research estimates the robotics market growing at 14% annually, reaching $110 billion by 2030-2035, up from $50 billion today

However, in practice, the growth dynamics are more complex.

NVE Corporation's (NVEC) product portfolio gives it direct exposure to the robotics market. It provides magnetic sensors and couplers that allow robotic arms measure their tilt and position. However, competition is high, and NVE will need to find its edge in a crowded market that values established long-term relationships.

Despite its small size, the company is profitable, allowing it to self-fund operations until it finds its niche and rhythm in the expanding robotics market. The company's 10 years of consecutive dividend payments mirror management's commitment to returning capital to shareholders, but that comes with a huge asterisk, as discussed in the following sections.

Reliability

A significant portion of NVE's sales is standardized sensors and couplers, with precise specs and mature manufacturing processes. They're not at the bleeding edge of technology, but that means they're walking a safe, trodden path, with fewer chances of making strategic risks. The probability that NVE falling from grace as Intel Corporation (INTC) did is small. This is a valuable trait for buy-and-hold investors. The keyword here is reliability.

The company does offer custom-made products to certain healthcare customers, including Abbott Laboratories (ABT). The qualification and verification needed to become a supplier for medical equipment are a testament to their quality.

NVE also appears in front of the curve when it comes to the protectionist regulatory trends, further enhancing its reliability appeal. While no company is immune to changing international trade policies, the fact that NVE runs its own clean room, where it etches and deposes wafers, gives it the necessary manufacturing infrastructure base to align its production footprint with current regulatory trends. The company expects between $700,000 and $1 million in manufacturing investment tax credits this fiscal year (p.15).

Revenue and Earnings Trends

NVE's relatively consistent performance is also a sign of its reliability. The company has been profitable since at least 2005. EBITDA has mostly hovered in the high teens range for the past 15 years, with an average of $17.5 million EBITDA annually. This consistency is demonstrated by the small difference between the EBITDA mean and the $13 million EBITDA low during the pandemic period. In other words, the standard deviation is low.
